name: PEG Parsing
summary: Parsing Expression Grammars (Bryan Ford, 2004) define syntax in terms of ordered-choice recognition. Naturally recursive descent; pairs with packrat memoization for guaranteed linear time at O(input × rules) memory cost.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Parsing Expression Grammars (Bryan Ford, 2004) define syntax in terms of recognition rather than generation. The choice operator is ordered: `A / B` tries `A` first, and only tries `B` if `A` fails. This eliminates ambiguity by construction — every valid input has exactly one parse tree.

PEG parsers are naturally recursive descent, with one function per grammar rule. Status (as of 2026-04): CPython has shipped `pegen`, its PEG parser generator, as the default parser since 3.9 (PEP 617); the old LL(1) parser was removed in 3.10. The PEG approach is the established CPython parser architecture, not an experimental side path.

sources.label: PEP 617 — New PEG parser for CPython

sections.title: Ordered choice and recognition
sections.content:
In PEG, earlier alternatives win: `A / B` succeeds with whichever of A or B matches first, and never reconsiders. This is recognition-based: the grammar describes how to recognise input, not how to generate strings. Ambiguity disappears — there is at most one parse — at the cost of making choice order semantically significant. A grammar author who reorders alternatives can change which inputs the grammar accepts.

This is a sharp contrast with CFG-based approaches that separate grammar description from parser conflict handling. PEGs bake disambiguation into the grammar itself.

sections.title: Packrat memoization
sections.content:
Naive PEG parsers can hit exponential time because backtracking re-explores the same input under multiple rules. Packrat parsing fixes this by memoizing every rule's result at every input position, guaranteeing linear time at the cost of O(input × rules) memory.

The practical limitation is the memo table: for a 1MB source file with 100 grammar rules, the memoization table is approximately 100MB. Various strategies exist (lazy memoization, bounded tables, selective memoization), but the space-time trade-off is fundamental.

Recent research extends packrat in two directions that matter for language tooling. One line handles left recursion through cycle detection and fixed-point iteration, removing the old rule that PEG grammars must be manually rewritten to remove left-recursive expression forms. Another line makes packrat memo tables incremental by storing parse results in edit-aware interval structures, so typical small edits avoid rebuilding the whole memo table.

sources.label: Warth, Douglass, Millstein — Packrat Parsers Can Support Left Recursion (PEPM 2008)

sections.title: Strengths and trade-offs
sections.content:
PEGs give grammar authors direct control over ambiguity resolution: there is no separate conflict-resolution phase, no need to engineer an LR table around shift/reduce conflicts. The grammar is the parser. This is appealing for languages where grammar evolution is constant and conflict diagnostics from generators (yacc, ANTLR) are hard to interpret.

The cost: choice order encodes meaning. A grammar author who reorders alternatives can silently change accepted language. Ordered-choice grammars are also less amenable to error recovery and ambiguity reporting than CFG-based engines, because the parser stops at the first matching alternative rather than enumerating all possible parses.

sources.label: PEP 617 — New PEG parser for CPython
Warth, Douglass, Millstein — Packrat Parsers Can Support Left Recursion (PEPM 2008)
